<system-reminder># System Information

Operating System: linux
Default Shell: 
Home Directory: /root
Current Working Directory: /root
Current Time: Fri Jul 17 2026 14:56:03 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)

# GIT STATUS
Current branch: 
Main branch (you will usually use this for PRs): 

Recent commits:</system-reminder>

# Qwik City Live Shared Counter over Server-Sent Events

## Background

You are building a real-time "live shared counter" widget for a Qwik City application. The application keeps a single shared integer counter in server memory. Every connected browser subscribes to a Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream and sees the counter change in real time. Any client can increment or decrement the shared counter, and the new value must be pushed to **every** currently connected subscriber (not just the client that made the change).

Everything must run locally with no external services: the pub/sub broker is an in-memory data structure inside the running server process, and the SSE stream and mutation endpoint are plain Qwik City endpoints.

## Requirements

* Maintain one shared, in-memory integer counter for the whole server process (initial value `0`).
* Provide an SSE endpoint that streams the current counter value to each connected client. On connection it must immediately emit the current value, and thereafter emit the new value every time the counter changes.
* Provide a mutation endpoint that adjusts the shared counter by a signed delta and broadcasts the resulting value to **all** currently connected SSE subscribers.
* Provide a plain endpoint that returns the current counter value as JSON.
* Render a home-page component that subscribes to the SSE stream from the browser, stores the latest value in reactive state, displays it, and unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. The subscription must be established with a browser `EventSource` created inside a client-only visible task, and the stream connection must be closed during cleanup.
* Multiple simultaneous SSE connections must all receive every broadcast update.

## Implementation Hints

* Use a Qwik City endpoint (`onGet`) with `requestEvent.getWritableStream()` to produce the `text/event-stream` response, and keep a shared registry (e.g. a `Set` of stream writers) in a server-only module so a single counter/subscriber list is shared across requests in the process.
* Use another endpoint (`onPost`) to mutate the shared counter and fan the new value out to every registered subscriber. Parse the JSON body with `requestEvent.parseBody()`.
* Keep the mutable state and broadcast logic in a module that is only ever imported by server endpoints so it is never dragged into the client bundle.
* Use `useVisibleTask$` for the browser-only `EventSource` subscription, store the value in a `useSignal`, and close the `EventSource` inside the task `cleanup` callback.
* Remove a subscriber's writer from the registry when its connection aborts (`requestEvent.signal`).

### Hard requirements (must match exactly)

* Project path: `/home/user/qwik-app`
* Start command: `npm run dev -- --port 3000 --host 0.0.0.0` (the Qwik City SSR dev server; a single persistent Node process so the in-memory counter and subscriber registry are shared across all requests). Port: `3000`.
* The home page (`GET /`) must render an element with `id="counter-value"` whose text content is the current counter value (a number). This element must update live in the browser as the counter changes.
* SSE stream endpoint: `GET /api/counter/stream`
  * Response `Content-Type` header must start with `text/event-stream`.
  * Each message must be a standard SSE data frame terminated by a blank line, where the data payload is JSON of the form `{"count": <integer>}` (i.e. a line `data: {"count": <integer>}` followed by an empty line).
  * Immediately after connecting, the endpoint must emit one frame carrying the current counter value.
* Mutation endpoint: `POST /api/counter`
  * Request body: JSON `{ "delta": <integer> }` (delta may be negative).
  * It must apply the delta to the shared counter, broadcast the new value to all connected SSE subscribers, and respond with status `200` and JSON body `{ "count": <integer> }` containing the updated value.
* Current-value endpoint: `GET /api/counter` must respond with status `200` and JSON body `{ "count": <integer> }` containing the current value.
* The counter is shared process-wide: a mutation from one client must be observable by every other client (via SSE) and via `GET /api/counter`.
I'll start by checking what's already in the environment to see if there's an existing project or if I need to set something up from scratch.

part.text: I'll scaffold a fresh Qwik City project using the npm create command with the basic starter template, running it non-interactively.

part.text: Project scaffolded successfully. Let me inspect the structure.
Good, this is the Qwik City "empty" starter with SSR-capable Node adapter set to none (means we just run dev server). Let me check package.json scripts and the routes/index.tsx file.

output.content:
{
  "name": "my-qwik-empty-starter",
  "description": "Blank project with routing included",
  "engines": {
    "node": "^18.17.0 || ^20.3.0 || >=21.0.0"
  },
  "engines-annotation": "Mostly required by sharp which needs a Node-API v9 compatible runtime",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "build": "qwik build",
    "build.client": "vite build",
    "build.preview": "vite build --ssr src/entry.preview.tsx",
    "build.types": "tsc --incremental --noEmit",
    "deploy": "echo 'Run \"npm run qwik add\" to install a server adapter'",
    "dev": "vite --mode ssr",
    "dev.debug": "node --inspect-brk ./node_modules/vite/bin/vite.js --mode ssr --force",
    "fmt": "prettier --write .",
    "fmt.check": "prettier --check .",
    "lint": "eslint \"src/**/*.ts*\"",
    "preview": "qwik build preview && vite preview --open",
    "start": "vite --open --mode ssr",
    "qwik": "qwik"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@builder.io/qwik": "^1.20.0",
    "@builder.io/qwik-city": "^1.20.0",
    "@eslint/js": "^9",

    "vite": "7.3.1",
    "vite-tsconfig-paths": "^4.2.1"
  }
}

part.text:
The dev script uses Vite in SSR mode, and I need to make sure it accepts the port and host flags when running. Now I'm setting up the server-side counter state module to manage a shared counter with subscribers, then creating the API routes to handle GET and POST requests for the counter endpoint and a separate streaming endpoint.

I should also update the home page component to use Qwik's lifecycle hooks and EventSource to listen for counter updates from the stream endpoint, with buttons to trigger increment and decrement actions. For the server-only module, I'll use the `.server.ts` suffix so Qwik City automatically prevents it from being bundled to the client. Let me verify the SSE implementation using Qwik City's request event API to get the writable stream for streaming responses.

matches.context: * Low-level access to write to the HTTP response stream. Once `getWritableStream()` is called,

part.text: Good, both APIs exist. Now let's create the server-only state module, the two API endpoints, and update the home page.

I need to set up two API endpoints: one for streaming counter updates via Server-Sent Events and another for standard GET and POST operations on the counter. I'll organize these under the routes directory with separate files for each endpoint, and I'll need to import the RequestHandler type from Qwik City.
I need to verify the structure and properties of the requestEvent.json file, particularly its signature and headers.

matches.context: readonly json: (statusCode: StatusCodes, data: any) => AbortMessage;

part.text: The signature matches, so calling `requestEvent.json()` should work fine—it internally throws an `AbortMessage` to halt further handler execution, so I don't need an explicit return statement.
I need to look at the actual implementation around that section to understand how the JSON calls handle responses and whether AbortMessage gets thrown or just returned.

The `json()` method in Qwik returns an `AbortMessage` synchronously and commits the response immediately, so I don't need to await or throw it—just calling `requestEvent.json(200, {...})` directly is the standard pattern. My current approach of returning early after `requestEvent.json(400, ...)` is fine defensively even though the response is already committed. Now I'm checking how to use `requestEvent.headers.set` for the stream response.

Since headers need to be set before calling `getWritableStream`, which is when the response actually commits, I should be good there. Next I'll update the home page component to add `useVisibleTask$` for client-side logic, set up an `EventSource` connection, add buttons for controls, and wire up the counter display with the proper element ID.
Now let's start the dev server and run functional tests.

part.text:
The SSR verification confirmed the counter element with id="counter-value" is rendering correctly with the initial "0" value, despite the HTML comments that were interfering with the grep pattern earlier. Now I need to test the browser-side behavior to ensure the element updates live as expected.

Since this environment doesn't have a traditional browser available, I can't easily run a headless browser like Playwright to verify the client-side hydration and EventSource streaming. The implementation relies on useVisibleTask$ and EventSource listening to "/api/counter/stream", which I've already confirmed works correctly on the backend. Rather than trying to set up a browser automation tool that likely isn't installed, I'll trust the code logic since the endpoints are functioning properly.

Let me also check the edge cases—specifically that the POST endpoint properly validates the delta parameter and returns a 400 error for non-integer values.

input.command: curl -s -i -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/counter -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"delta": "abc"}'; echo; curl -s -i -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/counter -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"delta": 1.5}'

part.text: Error handling works properly. Now let's do a quick browser-based end-to-end check to confirm the live UI update works with real EventSource in a browser, using the browser agent.

A Qwik City dev server is running locally at http://localhost:3000/. It serves a home page with an element `id="counter-value"` showing a live shared counter that updates via Server-Sent Events (SSE) from endpoint GET /api/counter/stream, and a mutation endpoint POST /api/counter with JSON body {"delta": <integer>} that changes the shared value.

Please do the following and report back results:
1. Navigate to http://localhost:3000/ and read the text content of the element with id="counter-value". Report its initial value.
2. Click the button labeled "+1" on the page three times (or find and click whatever increment button is present).
3. After clicking, wait ~1 second, then read the text content of #counter-value again and report the new value — it should have increased by 3 from the initial value, updating live WITHOUT a page reload (this confirms SSE push is working through the browser EventSource).
4. Open a second browser tab/page also navigated to http://localhost:3000/, and click the "-1" button once in this second tab. Then go back to the FIRST tab (without reloading it) and check whether #counter-value in the first tab updated to reflect the change made from the second tab. Report whether the first tab's value updated automatically without a manual reload.
5. Report any console errors observed during this process.

Submit your final findings using the attemptCompletion tool, including: initial value, value after 3 increments in tab 1, and whether tab 1 auto-updated when tab 2 changed the counter, and any errors encountered.

agent-browser - fast browser automation CLI for AI agents

Usage: agent-browser <command> [args] [options]

Start here (for AI agents):
  agent-browser skills get core --full

  Skills ship with the CLI (always version-matched) and include workflow

Core Commands:
  open <url>                 Navigate to URL

Configuration:
  agent-browser looks for agent-browser.json in these locations (lowest to highest priority):
    1. ~/.agent-browser/config.json      User-level defaults
    2. ./agent-browser.json              Project-level overrides
    3. Environment variables             Override config file values
    4. CLI flags                         Override everything

  Use --config <path> to load a specific config file instead of the defaults.
  If --config points to a missing or invalid file, agent-browser exits with an error.

  Boolean flags accept an optional true/false value to override config:
    --headed           (same as --headed true)
    --headed false     (disables "headed": true from config)
    --hide-scrollbars false (keeps native scrollbars visible in headless Chromium screenshots)

  Extensions from user and project configs are merged (not replaced).

  Example agent-browser.json:
    {"headed": true, "hideScrollbars": false, "proxy": "http://localhost:8080"}

  AGENT_BROWSER_JSON             JSON output
  AGENT_BROWSER_ANNOTATE         Annotated screenshot with numbered labels and legend

  AGENT_BROWSER_COLOR_SCHEME     Color scheme preference (dark, light, no-preference)
  AGENT_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD_PATH    Default download directory for browser downloads
  AGENT_BROWSER_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT  Default action timeout in ms (default: 25000)

  AGENT_BROWSER_ALLOWED_DOMAINS  Comma-separated allowed domain patterns
  AGENT_BROWSER_ACTION_POLICY    Path to action policy JSON file
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_ACTIONS  Action categories requiring confirmation
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_INTERACTIVE Enable interactive confirmation prompts

  HTTP_PROXY / HTTPS_PROXY       Standard proxy env vars (fallback if AGENT_BROWSER_PROXY not set)

  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_DIR   Default screenshot output directory
  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_QUALITY JPEG quality 0-100
  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_FORMAT Screenshot format: png, jpeg
  AI_GATEWAY_URL                 Vercel AI Gateway base URL (default: https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh)

Install:
  npm install -g agent-browser           # npm
  brew install agent-browser             # Homebrew
  cargo install agent-browser            # Cargo
  agent-browser install                  # Download Chrome (first time)

Examples:
  agent-browser open example.com
  agent-browser snapshot -i              # Interactive elements only
  agent-browser click @e2                # Click by ref from snapshot
  agent-browser fill @e3 "test@example.com"
  agent-browser find role button click --name Submit
  agent-browser get text @e1
  agent-browser screenshot --full
  agent-browser screenshot --annotate    # Labeled screenshot for vision models
  agent-browser wait 2000               # Wait for slow pages to settle
  agent-browser --cdp 9222 snapshot      # Connect via CDP port
  agent-browser --auto-connect snapshot  # Auto-discover running Chrome
  agent-browser stream enable            # Start runtime streaming on an auto-selected port
  agent-browser stream status            # Inspect runtime streaming state
  agent-browser --color-scheme dark open example.com  # Dark mode
  agent-browser --profile Default open gmail.com        # Reuse Chrome login state
  agent-browser --profile ~/.myapp open example.com    # Persistent custom profile
  agent-browser profiles                               # List available Chrome profiles
  agent-browser --session-name myapp open example.com  # Auto-save/restore state
  agent-browser chat "open google.com and search for cats"  # AI chat (single-shot)
  agent-browser chat                                        # AI chat (interactive REPL)
  agent-browser -q chat "summarize this page"               # Quiet mode (text only)

Command Chaining:
  Chain commands with && in a single shell call (browser persists via daemon):

  agent-browser open example.com && agent-browser snapshot -i
  agent-browser fill @e1 "user@example.com" && agent-browser fill @e2 "pass" && agent-browser click @e3
  agent-browser open example.com && agent-browser screenshot

input.result:
Findings from testing the Live Shared Counter app at http://localhost:3000/:

1. **Initial value**: `#counter-value` showed **2** on page load.

2. **After 3 clicks on "+1" (Tab 1)**: Waited ~1s after clicking, and the value updated to **5** (2 + 3 = 5), confirmed via SSE push without any page reload — the increments were reflected live.

3. **Cross-tab SSE sync test**:
   - Opened a second tab, also navigated to http://localhost:3000/.
   - Clicked "-1" once in Tab 2 (which had loaded its own SSE stream showing the current shared value of 5).
   - Switched back to Tab 1 (no reload) and waited ~1s.
   - Tab 1's `#counter-value` automatically updated from **5 → 4**, correctly reflecting the -1 change made in Tab 2.
   - **Result: YES — Tab 1 updated automatically without any manual reload**, confirming the SSE EventSource push mechanism correctly broadcasts shared state changes to all connected browser tabs.

4. **Console/errors check**: No console errors or page errors were observed. The only console output consisted of expected Qwik dev-mode debug logs (SSR dev mode banner, Vite HMR connection messages, Click-to-Source hint) — nothing indicating a failure.

**Summary**: SSE-based live counter updates work correctly both within a single tab (increment button clicks reflected instantly) and across multiple tabs (a mutation in one tab propagates via SSE to update the counter display in another tab in real time, with no reload required, and no console errors).

input.description: Verify live counter UI updates in browser
input.prompt:
A Qwik City dev server is running locally at http://localhost:3000/. It serves a home page with an element `id="counter-value"` showing a live shared counter that updates via Server-Sent Events (SSE) from endpoint GET /api/counter/stream, and a mutation endpoint POST /api/counter with JSON body {"delta": <integer>} that changes the shared value.

Please do the following and report back results:
1. Navigate to http://localhost:3000/ and read the text content of the element with id="counter-value". Report its initial value.
2. Click the button labeled "+1" on the page three times (or find and click whatever increment button is present).
3. After clicking, wait ~1 second, then read the text content of #counter-value again and report the new value — it should have increased by 3 from the initial value, updating live WITHOUT a page reload (this confirms SSE push is working through the browser EventSource).
4. Open a second browser tab/page also navigated to http://localhost:3000/, and click the "-1" button once in this second tab. Then go back to the FIRST tab (without reloading it) and check whether #counter-value in the first tab updated to reflect the change made from the second tab. Report whether the first tab's value updated automatically without a manual reload.
5. Report any console errors observed during this process.

Submit your final findings using the attemptCompletion tool, including: initial value, value after 3 increments in tab 1, and whether tab 1 auto-updated when tab 2 changed the counter, and any errors encountered.

output.result:
Findings from testing the Live Shared Counter app at http://localhost:3000/:

1. **Initial value**: `#counter-value` showed **2** on page load.

2. **After 3 clicks on "+1" (Tab 1)**: Waited ~1s after clicking, and the value updated to **5** (2 + 3 = 5), confirmed via SSE push without any page reload — the increments were reflected live.

3. **Cross-tab SSE sync test**:
   - Opened a second tab, also navigated to http://localhost:3000/.
   - Clicked "-1" once in Tab 2 (which had loaded its own SSE stream showing the current shared value of 5).
   - Switched back to Tab 1 (no reload) and waited ~1s.
   - Tab 1's `#counter-value` automatically updated from **5 → 4**, correctly reflecting the -1 change made in Tab 2.
   - **Result: YES — Tab 1 updated automatically without any manual reload**, confirming the SSE EventSource push mechanism correctly broadcasts shared state changes to all connected browser tabs.

4. **Console/errors check**: No console errors or page errors were observed. The only console output consisted of expected Qwik dev-mode debug logs (SSR dev mode banner, Vite HMR connection messages, Click-to-Source hint) — nothing indicating a failure.

**Summary**: SSE-based live counter updates work correctly both within a single tab (increment button clicks reflected instantly) and across multiple tabs (a mutation in one tab propagates via SSE to update the counter display in another tab in real time, with no reload required, and no console errors).

part.text: The browser test confirms full end-to-end functionality: live updates within a tab and real-time cross-tab synchronization via SSE broadcast, with no errors.
Server is still running cleanly with no errors. The implementation is complete and fully verified.

input.result: Built a Qwik City app at `/home/user/qwik-app` implementing a real-time shared counter over SSE. **Structure:** - `src/lib/counter-store.server.ts` — server-only module (`.server.ts` suffix guarantees it's never bundled to the client) holding the single in-memory `counter` (starts at `0`) and a `Set` of SSE `WritableStreamDefaultWriter`s. Exposes `getCount`, `applyDelta` (mutates + broadcasts to all writers), `subscribe`/`unsubscribe`, and `encodeCountFrame` (builds `data: {"count": N}\n\n` SSE frames). - `src/routes/api/counter/index.ts` — `onGet` returns `{ "count": N }` JSON (200); `onPost` parses `{ "delta": N }` via `requestEvent.parseBody()`, validates it's an integer, applies it via `applyDelta` (which broadcasts), and responds `{ "count": N }` (200), or `400` on invalid input. - `src/routes/api/counter/stream/index.ts` — `onGet` sets `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, gets a writer from `requestEvent.getWritableStream()`, registers it via `subscribe()` (which immediately returns and emits the current value), and removes/closes the writer when `requestEvent.signal` aborts (client disconnect). - `src/routes/index.tsx` — home page with `useSignal` for the count, a `useVisibleTask$` that opens `new EventSource("/api/counter/stream")`, updates the signal `onmessage`, and closes the `EventSource` in the task's `cleanup`. Renders `{count.value}` plus `+1`/`-1` buttons that `POST` to `/api/counter` (the new value arrives back through the SSE push rather than being set locally, so all clients — including the one that clicked — stay in sync via the same code path). **Verification performed:** - `GET /api/counter` and `POST /api/counter` (including negative deltas and validation of non-integer bodies → 400) tested via `curl`, confirming shared state persists process-wide. - `GET /api/counter/stream` confirmed to return `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and immediately emit the current value as a `data: {"count": N}` frame. - Two concurrent `curl` SSE connections were opened simultaneously; a subsequent `POST /api/counter` mutation was broadcast to and received by **both** connections, confirming multi-subscriber fan-out. - End-to-end browser test (via the browser agent): loaded the home page, clicked "+1" three times in one tab and observed `#counter-value` update live (SSE, no reload) from 2→5; opened a second tab, clicked "-1" there, and confirmed the first tab's `#counter-value` automatically updated 5→4 without a manual reload — no console errors observed. The dev server is running in the background via `npm run dev -- --port 3000 --host 0.0.0.0` (job id `eedc6127-74a8-4df9-965d-a9314d99aede`) and is currently serving the app at port 3000, satisfying the single persistent Node process requirement for the shared in-memory state.
